questionText: Prove that the product of any three consecutive even numbers is divisible by $48$.

steps.description: Represent the three consecutive even numbers.
steps.explanation: Consecutive even numbers step up by $2$, so starting from $2n$ they are $2n$, $2n+2$ and $2n+4$.
The claim concerns the product of the three even numbers.
Each even factor contributes a $2$: $2n=2\cdot n$, $2n+2=2(n+1)$, $2n+4=2(n+2)$.
Multiplying the three $2$s gives an explicit factor of $8$ times a product of three consecutive integers.
Since we already have a factor of $8$, it remains to show $n(n+1)(n+2)$ is divisible by $6$.
Two consecutive integers include an even one, so the triple product is even.
Checking each residue shows one of the three consecutive factors is always a multiple of $3$.
Divisible by $2$ and by $3$ with $\gcd(2,3)=1$, so the triple product is divisible by $6$.
Divisibility by $6$ lets us write the triple product as $6m$.
Replacing the triple product with $6m$ turns the factor $8$ into $48$.
A number equal to $48$ times an integer is a multiple of $48$.
No special assumption on $n$ was made, so the result holds for every triple of consecutive even numbers.
Hence the product of any three consecutive even numbers is divisible by $48$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $(2n)(2n+2)(2n+4)=8\,n(n+1)(n+2)$. As $n(n+1)(n+2)$ is a product of three consecutive integers it is divisible by $6$, say $6m$, so $P=48m$. Hence $48\mid P$. $\blacksquare$
